metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_5345
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Figure 3: The deubiquitinating enzyme USP5 interacts with Cav3.2 channels.

Figure 6: USP5 regulation of Cav3.2 channels in dorsal root ganglia neurons and dorsal horn synapses.
"To determine if the effects of USP5 occurred via Cav3.2 T-type channels, we designed Tat peptides to competitively disrupt USP5 binding to the Cav3.2 domain III-IV linker (Figure 6A).
...
To test if these synaptic T-type channels are regulated by USP5, we performed miniature excitatory postsynaptic current recordings from spinal cord slices from the lumbar region (Figures 6D and 6E).
...
One third of the nickel-sensitive cells displayed a statistically significant increase in interevent interval in response to the Tat-3.2-III-IV peptide (which was quantified and compared to the nonresponders) (Figure 6E).
...
To determine if USP5 regulation occurs in synaptic nerve terminals from afferent fibers rather than excitatory interneurons, we examined the effect of the Tat-3.2-III-IV peptide on T-type channel-mediated events in spinal cord slices evoked by paired pulse stimulation of the dorsal root entry zone. As shown in Figures 6F and 6G, the peptide resulted in a statistically significant increase in paired pulse ratio but not a statistically significant decrease in evoked EPSC amplitude.
...
Altogether, these experiments indicate that USP5 regulation of Cav3.2 channels occurs both in cell bodies and in synaptic nerve terminals arising from DRG neurons, which collectively would result in an enhancement of afferent pain signaling.". Experimental description: Figure 6 legend: "Examples of calcium fluorescence measurements in acutely dissociated mouse DRG neurons before and after application of either the Tat-3.2-III-IV or C terminus control peptide (Tat-3.2-CT) in response to a 25 mM KCl-induced depolarization."
